title: Dukkah
subtitle: Egyptian nut, seed, and spice mix
excerpt: Hazelnuts or almonds toasted with sesame seeds and ground spices, then coarsely pounded to a rubble of crunch and fragrance. Eaten by dipping bread first in olive oil, then in the dukkah. Also used as a crust for lamb or chicken, or scattered over hummus, yoghurt, and roasted vegetables.
sourceNotes: Dukkah (also dukka or duqqa) is the Egyptian nut and spice condiment, eaten as a dip and used as a coating and seasoning. Every Egyptian family has a dukkah recipe. The base of hazelnuts or almonds with sesame, cumin, and coriander is consistent; additions such as dried mint, fennel seed, and dried chilli vary by household. It is made by pounding in a mortar rather than grinding finely, the coarse, rubble-like texture is characteristic.

content.text: Toast the nuts and seeds separately. They require different amounts of time and burn at different speeds. The hazelnuts take the longest (8 to 10 minutes in a dry pan); the sesame seeds toast in 2 to 3 minutes. Combine them only after both are toasted and cooled.

Pound rather than grind: the texture should be coarse and irregular, with some pieces nearly whole and others almost powdery. A food processor produces a uniform crumb that lacks character. A mortar and pestle or a brief pulse in a food processor in short bursts is the correct approach.

Toast the {{hazelnuts}} in a dry pan over medium heat for 8 to 10 minutes, shaking the pan frequently, until golden brown and fragrant. Tip onto a plate to cool. In the same pan, toast the {{sesame-seeds}} for 2 to 3 minutes until golden. Tip onto the same plate. Once cool, combine the hazelnuts, sesame seeds, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} in a mortar. Pound to a coarse, irregular rubble, not a powder. Alternatively, pulse very briefly in a food processor 5 to 6 times. Taste and adjust salt. Store in an airtight jar. Serve with olive oil and bread for dipping.

items.fix: Pulse in 3 to 4 short bursts maximum. Finish in a mortar if needed. A fine dukkah loses the textural contrast that makes it interesting
Use fresh nuts and seeds. Toasting amplifies both good and bad flavour. Dukkah should be made in small batches and consumed within 3 weeks
items.cause: Hazelnuts or sesame seeds already old before toasting

items.fix: Toast over medium, not high, heat. Shake the pan every 30 seconds. Hazelnuts go from golden to burnt quickly, stay with them throughout
items.cause: Heat too high or not shaken frequently enough

content.text: Dukkah is the Egyptian condiment that has had the most impact internationally. In Australia and New Zealand it became popular in the 1990s through Egyptian immigrant communities, then spread to restaurant menus worldwide. In Egypt it is eaten daily at breakfast and as a pre-meal snack. Bread dipped in olive oil, then in dukkah, is the Egyptian version of bread and butter before a meal. It is also used as a crust for lamb cutlets, mixed into yoghurt, scattered over hummus, and used to season roasted vegetables.
